TOND CHLIDNA I. 


O’Curry, MS. Mat. 306, places Tond Clidna in or near Clonakilty Bay, Co. Cork, 
but according to O'Donovan it is to be sought somewhat further west in Glandore 
Bay: see his note FM v. 1648. 

The story of Clidna and Ciabán is told in Acallam na Senérach, where this 
poem is introduced ; it is translated in Sil. Gad. ii. 201. For the readings of the 
Laud and Lismore texts of the Acallam I have relied on Stokes (Ir. Text iv. 109 
with notes): Mr. Plummer has kindly supplied me with a collation of the copy in 
Rawlinson B 487. 

5. the: see note on p. 68, 65 ante: in the present instance the texts vary 
between de and te. 

8. Ciabén: the prose story calls him Iuchna Ciab-faindech. 

21. Teite, daughter of Ragamain, and the Strand called after her, are 
mentioned in the Acallam story. 

31. do neoch : this usage is illustrated by Atkinson, Pass. and Hom. 817 a. 

33. Ildathach and his two sons are not referred to in the prose Dindsenchas ; but 
in the Acallam the son of the King of India and the son of the King of Greece 
share Ciaban’s adventure: these are, no doubt, the same persons in a later dress. 

37-40. These ships must bave pursued Clidna when she eloped from the Land 
of Promise. 

39. chongaid seems to be put metri gratia for congab, which may mean either 
‘seizure’ or ‘assembly.’ The line may perhaps mean ‘that was not a gathering 
without a spear,’ i.e. they were well armed.

CARN UI NEIT. 


Hooan places this Carn near Mizen Head in south-west Cork. According to 
Stokes it is said to be the Old Head of Kinsale, but he does not name his 
authority. 

The extremely complicated metre has the usual effect of making the poem 
difficult to construe. It is written in Rindaird (the metre of Félire Oenguseo), 
and there is a lavish profusion of rhyme. Besides the obligatory couplet-rhyme 
(3 and 4 of each stanza), there is regularly one internal rhyme, sometimes two, 
between 1 and 2, and also between 3and4. The end-wonds of 1 and 3 also 
assonate with the couplet-rhymes, except when they themselves rhyme with a 
word in the following line. Alliteration is general, but not quite regular. 

‘The personages mentioned belong to the ‘Tuatha De Danann ; their names occur 
e.g- in Cath Maige Tured: see Rev. Celt. xii. 63, ete. The subject is a trial of 
strength in magic arts between Brees and Lug mao Ethlenn, in which Bress gets 
the worst of it: the geiss which he has laid upon the men of Ireland, as stated in 
the prose story, is turned against himself, and he dies of the results. It is an 
example of the comic grotesquerie which takes the place of humour in the older 
legends of Ireland. The story is referred to by Fland Mainistrech in his poem 
on the Tuatha Dé, LL 11 a 39-40. 

1-2. bhada . . . gessi are written for bwadaib . . . gessid, to mark the rhyme: so 
35, for ratha. 

With gessi grdda cp. ba geis tria ngrád ngentlidi, p. 70, 48. 

6. This pedigree is given also in BB 34a 66 (and 23). Neit mac Indai maic 
Alldai maic Thaid maic Tabairnd, Hence I restore ba 4Alldai; for the construo- 
tion of. sciath ba Ddire, p. 122, 29. In 7 read with all texts except L is maic 
Tabwirn: cf. p. 140, 6; p. 338, 22, where the uss. have mo ithe 7 mo Bregoin 
(mo itha ot m breoguin H), also LL 206 5 41, 42, 44, 45.

CROTTA CLIACH. 


O'Donovan (Topogr. Poems, Ixxxiii), followed by Hogan, Onom., says that this 
is an old name for the Galtee Mountains. 

The legend of Cliach and Bodb’s daughter is told in Leabhar Breac 2426, 
where it is followed by two other legends, accounting for two names of the lake 
which burst forth when the ground opened under Cliach (see O’Curry, MS. Mat. 
426). These two legends are not in any other way connected with the fairy 
harper: the author of our poem has tried to supply a connexion with one of 
them in lines 17-20. 

3. The phrase fri gorm-greis ngrinde is obscure. I withdraw the rendering 
offered, but cannot suggest any probable explanation. 

8. I should now prefer to render this line ‘ the amart of womanly desire was 
', or ‘was constraining (the maiden).’ For brig applied to the physical 
» cf. andhaindi na brigi tochlaigthi, quoted in Contrib.

síd ar F. and sid for F. 

11. fuired: the meaning is doubtful: O'R. has fuireadh ¢ preparation, feast,’ 
cf. glóire glan-fuirid, p. 288, 44; dobadh fuireadh Bachmarcach obuir Eirinn 
Shulang (corrupt f) Misc. Celt. Soc. 1849, p. 412, 16 tr.‘ E. was ready.’ But 
in these instances we probably have to do with fulired (= fiir) verbal noun af 
Siirim (prototonic of foririm), 

14, alégaib: the poet seems to conceive Cliach as attended by followers from 
bis own sid, cf. 6, 7. In the LBr. version of the story he is not a fer side at all 
but the barper of a human king, Smirdub mac SmAil, and he seems to go on his 
adventure alone, 

$ sir-chacht, they were benumbed with fear.

such eights.

or amra brige (see Contrib.).

22. cen meirbe: rather, ‘ without feebleness.”

CEND FEBRAT. 


Ture name is often written Cend Abrat. It belonged to a part of Sliab Riach, 
now Slieve Reagh, lying to the south-east of Kilmallock, on the confines of 
Limerick and Cork: O'Donovan FM 186: Suppl. to O'R. s.v. ceann abpoc; Ir. 
T. iv, index: see further Wars of G. and G., cxxzvii n., olx n. Of the heroes 
mentioned in this poem, some (if not all) belong to the Clann Dedad or Érainn of 
Munster. Febra, according to the prose version, was brother of Deda uuc Sin,

and was slain by Chin mac Deirg (26), who was killed in turn by Garbán son of 
Deda (33). Lugaid Laigde (40) was one of the sons of Dáire Doimthech (or 
Sirchrechtach) (42), aneestor of the Dairfine : for his pedigree see the Genealogy of 
Coreu Laifg]de in Mise. Celt. Soc. (1849), pp. 25, 57: Fianaigecht, 28; LL 325 « 
562g. As to Dáire'a sons, see LL as above; Cóir Anm. § 68; Misc. Celt. Soc. 25 ; 
ds. of Carn Máil ibid. 67 og. Dodera (46)is the jester of Lugaid mac Con, who was 
killed in the battle of Cenn Febrat (Rev. Celt. xiii. 441, ef. Fianaig. 35). 

9-12. I would now translate these lines somewhat differently.‘ The sound of 
the wind lulled me to sleep with vacant mind; and there I met with pure poetic 
lore among the hands of warriors’ : (literally, ‘it was a meeting with purity of 
wisdom’). A contrast is intended between the war-stained warriors and the pure 
visions which are revealed to the poet ‘in a wise passiveness.’ With the whole 
passage compare the opening of Cúirt an Mheadhén-Oidhche. 

18. Cathgus is perhape a proper name, as in ZCP viii. 297, 10.

place-names.

Dáire, viz. Eithne, Maer, and Mugain,’ but I have not been able to find elsewhere 
any mention of his wives. There are several women bearing these names. 

60. om ehsias: i.e. it aprang up naturally, without digging. 

61. in iain: Metr. Gl. and O'C1. have fien .i. caí: perhaps then dian is 
intended to suggest Lugaid mac Con.

Knocklong in Co. Limerick,’ Onom. Cend Aife has not been identified. 

73, Who is ‘the son of Fland’? Presumably the author himeelf. His name 
is given in the superscription of L as Mac Raith Ga Pasin. Nothing further is 
known of him under that name; but Meyer, Primer of Metrics, quotes the name 
of a poet Mac Raith mao Flaind mac Rchthigern, from BB 183 6 33, where it oseuns 
in a genealogical account of Dá] Cais: the passage runs thus: De mae dee aero 





p. 234] NOTES. 519 


ag Ceindetigh, cdie maic dib aga fuil sit i. Brian otait aif m Briain ote., 7 
Eehthigern otait hui Eehtigern . is do rob ua Mac Raith fils i. Mac Raith maio 
Floind maic Echthigern. This poet therefore belongs to the end of the tenth or first 
half of the eleventh century. He may be identical with the author of our poem : 
however the strange ‘Ga Pasin’ is to be explained, it is not a genuine Irish 
patronymic. 

75. techta: plural of techtad, verbal noun of techtaim, Cf. p. 366, 19, for

topur techta; LL 295 5 29 techta in talman. The ‘ possessions’ must be the mounds 
where the heads are buried. 

It is also possible that ria (re) tehte may mean ‘before going (vanishing in 
death),” techta being a plural form of teo¢, as dula of dul. 


CEND CUIRRIG [CURRECH LIFE]. 


The title of this poem is wrongly given in the text: it is the dindsenchas of 
Cend Cuirrig, which is, according to O'Donovan, Suppl. O'R., “a townland on 
the south side of the Suir, in Co. Waterford, about one quarter mile from Killaloan 
old church, in the barony of Iffa and Offa east.” See also the authorities quoted 
in Onom. It is therefore distinct from Cuirrech Life, the Curragh of Kildare. It 
is associated both in the prose and in our poem with Badammair (Bodamair) or Réth 
Badamrach, which is said to be near Cahir in Co. Tipperary: see O'Don, Suppl., 
Onom., and Rev. Celt. xv 444. The origin of Finn’s feud with Currech and his 
family is set forth in the prose, and also, more fully, in the tale Bruiden Atha, 
‘ed. Meyer, Rev. Celt. xiv 242, where the slaying of Teite and her husband Find 
mac Regamna is also narrated. 

1-4. This stanza is quoted Ir. T. iii 32, as an example of dien airseng. It is 
there ascribed to Cáilte. 

2. rodamain. This is a conjectural reading, intended as verbal noun of demnain 

«I subdue’ with intensit But the rodamair of S3,H should be kept: it is 
a prototonic form of roddmair (damim), with the accent on the first syllable. 
‘Translate ‘there are few kings to whom he yielded.” 

6. bdn-bidbad, i.e. Badammair, Finn’s mistress, whom Cuirreoh slew. Grief for 
her death made her friends gather for revenge, but it was a single hero, Finn, that 
killed Currech. 

didba here means ‘ culprit’ or “ victim ' rather than ‘enemy.’ 

10. For cifath ‘ phalanx’ see Contrib.; but here I think it means the wicker- 
work shield, whose sharp edges or corners dripped with blood. 

13-14. Fothad Cananne was Currech’s half-brother (see the prose), and would 
be expected to avenge him. I cannot explain roselt. The form ocours in the 
following passages: ZUP vi 269 rosalt ó gasith 7 gréin ‘sheltered from wind and

TEMAIR LUACHRA. 


Ture place was somewhere near Castleisland in Co. Kerry, Onom. : cf. 
O'Donovan at FM p. 1731. Line 40 says that Loch Léin (the lakes of Killarney ) 
‘sprang up ‘over Luachair,’ so that this district must have extended along the 
shores of the lake, which is about fifteen miles due south of Castleisland. It was 
Part of the region occupied by the Clann Dedaid, or Erainn of Munster, when 
driven back by the increasing power of the Eoganachta. The mes ascribe the 
poem to the legendary Fintan mac Béchra, who names himself in 41. One of 
the poems on Tara is attributed to him, Metr. Ds. i. 4, 41... iw

TIPRA SENGARMNA. 


‘Ture well must be one of the sources of the river Féil or Féile (see line 96). 
There were two rivers of the name in Kerry, according to O'Donovan, note on 
FM i. 87; they are confused in Onom. sv. Aba Féile. We are concerned with 
the northern Feale, which flows past Abbeyfeale and Listowel in north Kerry, 
and discharges into the sea a little south of Ballybunion, It is fed principally 
from the mountains north of Castleisland, eo that the Well of Sengarman must be 
somewhere on their northern slopes. 

The story is a continuation of the feud between Find mac Cumaill and 
Currech Lif, which forms the subject of the legend of Cond Currig. 

The poem s attributed in most uss to Fergus Fin-bél, a poet of the Fianna 
(eee Ir. T. iv index).

105-8. desberat, focherdat (musnagat most uss), fuaprait. The was retain 
the conjunct endings in the deuterotonic forms, but discard them in the prototonic.

rangell seems to mean ‘held him as a pledge,’ or ‘exacted a pledge from him’ :

127. i ruth gait: ruth is dat. of roth‘ a wheel, circle,’ here a noose. A

“they bound.’ The other uss. read fogniset gait ‘they committed robbery’ and 
aniset ‘ they tormented.” 











FINDGLAIS. 


‘Tus is the name of a stream flowing down from the Sliab Miss range, a little 
south of Tralee, among which stood Cathair Chonréi. The story of Curió's 
death by the treachery of Blathnat is told in Aided Conréi ed. Best, Ériu ii, 20, 
from YBL (quoted as Y on pp. 254-5). See also Brinna Firchertne, ed. Meyer, 
ZCP iii. 40. The first two stanzas of our poem are introduced into these 
compositions. 

1. Roort: it is perhaps better to take this as passive “ Blathnat was killed in 
the fighting.” Brinna Firch. (et. 8) has romert. 

2, This Airget-Glenn must be a valley among the Slieve Mish mountains: it 
is not in Hogan’s Onom. 

4. rodaemidir : is this intended as dep. pret. of aswidiur ? Brine Firch., 
stanza 8, reads dáig ba maith donderuidir. 

6. imwar: cf. note on 134, 22. Aid. Con. and Br. F. (st. 33) read comrae. 

8. In Br. F. ‘Ai Laind Chind Bera assin Rind.’ In Keating's version of 
the story (ii. 222-6), Blathuat is carried off to Ulster, after the death of Curéi, and 
Rind Cind Bera would consequently be somewhere in the North: but the old tale 
makes her death take place during the fighting at Cathair Conréi. (The three 
entries in Onom. under Rind Cind Bera all relate to our tale.) 

9. Fer Breoach is called Fer Beorach, Cursi’s charioteer, in Aid. Con. § 9, 
Senfiacail in mentioned ibid. § 7, and in Br. F. st. 18, Fergsire in Br. F. st. 14.

‘Tux exact situation of this Carn has not been determined, but it was somewhere

eo Adain: other instances of this phrase are p. 372, 81 Rolen oo huain in gairm 
glan “the name stuck perpetually’: p. 378. 1 Atbér frib co hiain ; BB 398 a 16 
Aindat na druidi co huain: Archiv iii. 232, ar do maith co mór, ar do flaith cen lón, 
co hopunn co huain tue dam topur dér Silv. Gad. i. 105. 10, ar laich ar ngadair co 
huain ag fagail unman eamchlwain. In this last instance O'Grady renders ‘at 
their discretion,’ Stokes (Ir. T. iv. index) ‘leisurely,’ which would be co n-Gain, 
‘The meaning suggested by a comparison of the above passages is ‘to the end, 
completely.’ Perhaps wain, verbal noun of oidim, which means properly ‘loan,’ 
and secondarily ‘leisure,’ ‘delay,’ may have also developed the sense of ‘the term 
when a loan falls due,’ ‘échéance,” and hence generally “a fixed term or date.’

LUIMNECH. 


Tun namo survives in the town and county of Limerick; but it originally 
denoted the whole estuary of the Shannon (Onom.: FM 661). In this poem it is 
applied both to the town (9-16) and to the river (47, 64). ‘The poem is founded 
on the tale Cophur in dá Mucoida. Rind and Faebur are the two swine herds 
Friuch and Rucht, in one of their transformations: see Windisch’s edition, Ir. 
T. iii. 243 and 245. ‘The enoounter of the two in the Shannon is briefly described 
in the Egerton text, ibid. p. 237. 

2. nda has perhaps here the sense of ‘fame (famous place or legend)’ 
suggested in Ir. T. iv. index: of. Wi. TBC index ; O'Dav. 423, where & nes fer 








I have not found any parallel to the construction of this 
passage. In modern Irish drwidim le means ‘I approach,’ the objective being 
indicated by the preposition. 

6. rosrethad may apply in a concrete sense to the river; but cf. ein 
rosrethad sith, p. 366, 11: ee fail depends on labrad, rosrethad. 

7. cen tairbirt may mean ‘ unsubdued ' or ‘ unsurpassed.’ 

11. sattail seems = sotal, ' proud.’ 

14. na loingss, 'o the fleet.’

productive of sound, L. got its name.’ For twillmech, see note on 68. 

23. lll may be either gen. or aco. of fell, which is both masc. (Wi.) and fem. 
(Three Shafts, index : line 39 of this poem). 

26. What may be the precise meaning of gié-chwirr is not easy to say: corr 
is applied to anything pointed or prominent: render perhaps ‘ of the bright 
spear-point.” 

21. fri forga figes cf. p. 84, 4, for figed feirg.

as = de.fedil ; but here and at p. 298, 11 it is a substantive.

adraleat : is this the sigmatic pres. subj. of adsligin (in the sense ‘I attempt’)

demnigthe is the imper. of demnigim (deponent), used as a cheville.

68. twillmech in a derivative of tnillem * earning’ or ‘wages’: the adjective 
means ‘able to earn, productive, profitable.’ The sense here is that the warriors, 
having lost their shields, are no longer worth their wages. Wi. gives for ¢wiliem 
the meaning ‘ Hinzufiigen’ (as well as ‘Verdionen, Lohn’): Stokes’ Ir. T. iv, 
Glossary, has ¢utdlmech “ bountiful,’ and in Mart. Oeng. gloss. translates tuillem 
by ‘enhancement, addition.’ Meyer, Tec. Corm. gloss. has tuidlmech ‘adding, 
ineressing.’ I think these meanings belong rather to twilied, and its derivative 
tuilttech (Metr. Ds. i. 6, 17). In Fél. Oeng. Prol. 65, I would translate ar fir- 
thwillem buide “in reward for fair earning of thanks,’ and in Teo. Corm., p. 30, 
51, tuillmecha augrai ‘ fit to earn (nothing but) strife.’ 





SLIGE DALA. 


As to this and the other roads enumerated in lines 41-8, see O'Donovan, Introd. 
to L. na gCeart Iviii. According to him Slige Dála was the great south-western 
road of Ireland, which extended from the south-western side of Tara in the 
direction of Ossory. Slige Aasail was a western road extending from the bill of 
Tara in the direction of Loch Uair (Lough Owel) near Mullingar. Slige 
Midluachra was the northern road freyuently mentioned in Tain Bó Cualnge. 
Slige Cualann extended from ‘Tara in the direction of Dublin and Bray. Slige 
Mor was the great western road, the lie of which is defined by the Eacir Riada, a 
line of gravel hills extending from Dublin to Medraige, near Galway. See also 
FM 123. 

The prose version edited by Stokes refers to Airne Fingein ; and the particulars 
it gives about the roads are taken from that authority: see the text in Aneed. ii. 4. 
Some of these are details omitted from the poem and from the prose version in 
LL (ed. in Silv. Gad. ii. 477). 

4. mandir can hardly be referred to mandar ‘dissolution,’ which belongs 
either to the o-decl. or w-decl., see O’Dav. 1255: cf. cen mannur LL 163 a 6, 
BB 65 a 46. It is probably mainder ‘ enclosure,’ properly “ pen for sheep or cattle,’ 
used metaphorically: cf. Three Shafts 75, 17, i mainnir mAiothaomaigh an 
domhain-se; 209, 14, i mainnir na mimhéine. ord in the same way means either 
“a cattle-pen’ or “a ring of fighters.’ 

5. Dale. Lgives the nom. as Dalo here and in 13, the gen. as Dala in 2, 25, 
43, 84. The other uss. vary. In 17 (which is not in L) four uss. have Dala, 
three Dale.

predicative adjective is sometimes indeclinable even in early Middle Irish; see

it included Thurles, Roscrea, and Birr.

SINANN I. 


‘Tuzs legend of the Shannon traces its origin to the Well of Segais in the Land of 
Promise. This is the fountain from which the Boyne also is supposed to spring : 
see p. 26,9. The legends are very similar. References to the mystic hazels and 
the salmon of knowledge will be found in Rev. Celt. xv. 457 : see also Ir. T. 
195 § 35.

SLIAB N-ECHTGA I. 


Wr now pase from Munster into Connaught. Slieve Aughty is a range of hills 
extending westward from Lough Derg. 

The metre of this poem is Rindaird: see introductory note to ds. Carn i Neit, 
p. 216, As often happens, the complicated metrical system is responsible for 
various irregularities in grammar. 

11. dédait : see note on p. 272, 30. 

daig is a conjecture. I do not understand L's dáig, and the reading of the 
other ues sacrifices alliteration. 

13. a lenmus = asa noidendacht in the prose. O'R. has leandop 
‘childiahness.? 

16, Sid Nenta is well known in Irish legend as the home of Midir 
terrestrial locality is uncertain. 

16. rogeita is required by the rhyme, so guide must be regarded as plural. 

20, Cf. LL 208 bs: rddid fir 7 fegaid. 

22, dodrim in not a good thyme for the disyllabic dtim. 

dag-dáin: the uss show deg-dan, and in 24 samneir (L samnar). But in order 
to asednate with dínd-móir in 23, the rhyme-words must have a long palatal 








p. 804] NOTES. 581 


diphthong in the second syllable. I have therefore written dag-ddin, although 
the regular plural would be dag-ddna; and sam-ndir, which I take to be a 
compound similar to sam-there at p. 22, 279. 

26. Alliteration is wanting : read perhaps eo ndil-deb. 

37. dia glan-fir: this conjecture gives a satisfactory assonance ; the glanuir, 
&o., of the wes do not, and are unintelligible to me. For the phrase compare 
Metr. Ds. ii 48, 39, maith dia fir in lá labraim “ good in sooth is the day,’ &c. 

29, filite, literally ‘folded, twisted’: O'R. has pillce ‘treacherous.’ The 
word occurs in LL 201 a 15, na teora fogla filte; Laws iv 290, 14 ata fodsilingud 
Allt for fini na foduer (tr. ‘ manifold’), BB 407 a 20 Ie desin adbearar forainm 
Segar fille. 

30. do serb-lus. The best was (LRB) read thred-tus, but this does not give 
alliteration. The phrase may mean ‘for bitter increase of his greed’; but 
Mr. Purton suggests that do ue may be used like af-los ‘on account of,’ “by the 
help of.” 

31. muilte: gen. of tuilled used adjectivally. 

35. thoirthe for thoirthib, to rhyme with coibehe. 

47. foird dder means, I take it, land cultivated by serfs. 

49. airle ndualais: of. p. 284, 100, a ndúalwa a ndind-senchas; LL 213 a 60, 
a haided can dualus ndil. The form dualgue is commoner : see glossaries to Laws, 
Pass. and Hom., Three Shafts, Ir. T. iv. dual is “what is according to kith or 
kind’ (Dinneen), so that airle dúalaís would be ‘such counsel as might be 
expected from him.” But perhups it simply means ‘lawful counsel.’ 

aailie may be ‘affection’ (so Contrib.) or ‘ deceitfulness.” 

63. feis in the verbal noun ‘sleeping with.” 

54. ergnue: see Contrib. airgnas. 

As to fuithir, see Stokes’ note, Rev. Celt. xv 458.

SLIAB N-ECHTGA II. 


Or this poem there are two copies, one in the Book of Leinster, the other in the 
Yellow Book of Lecan. In the latter manuscript it occurs in n part of the volume 
which hus otherwise nothing to do with the Dindsenchas, and is introduced by a 
tale which explains the heading of the LL copy, Fland macLondin post mortem 
nam cecinit. An abstract of this story will be found in MC ii 99, 100, where 
lines 93-108 of the poem are translated. As O’Curry suggests, the real author of 
the poem is very likely Mac Ling (+ 1016), to whom MacLonsin is supposed to 
recite it. Mac Liag’s patron, Brian Boroime, is addressed by name in line 109. 
TODD LECTURE SERIES, VOL.x. 2N 





582 NOTES. [p. 304 


The textual problems involved are very difficult. There are continual points 
of disagreement, more or lees serious, between the two copies; the order of the 
stanzas is different; L has four lines, 67-70, which are not in Y, and Y has 
fourteen stanzas, 45-56, 81-120, 133-136, which do not occur in L. A further 
complication is introduced by the list of names entitled dindsencas Behtga in 
LL 170a; this is based on a text of lines 1-72 which must have been closely 
similar to (but not identical with) that of L. I have not found it possible to 
follow either L or Y throughout. 


The introductory tale in Y (col. 916 = facs. 196) runs as follows :—

timpanaich meic Linc 7 timpanach meic Lonain roime he 7 dobai ac mac Liac iar 
n-eo meic Lonain. Docbuaid mac Liac do indesigid Briain dia acallaim 7

fodeas co Luimneach ocus da puitric dec lais con a mbiad dingbala leo. Uair is 
da radaro deo atat an-Echtgi 7 puitric noibead in cach radare dibsin. Feacht 
and tra dochuadar fodeas 7 rosuidsedar i n-aroile enue inti .i. ceann Crochain 7

Tirachtach : damad he mac Lonain nobeith sunn nobiad sici a fis dindseanchais 
each inaid sund. Adbert mac Liag: Gabair sut 7 crochair he. Rochunnig Irech- 
tach dal co maidin 7 tuead do can a chrochad, 7 rothraisc an aidchi sin co toracht 
anum Floind meic Lonain dia chobair. o drechtadar madan moch iarnabarach,

“There were three learned posts of Connaught, Mac Liac and Mac Coise and 
Fland mac Lonain, that is, the son of God, the son of Man, and the son of the 
Demon. Fland mac Lonain was called the son of the Demon, for his covetousness 
and -urliness ; for he never entered a house without causing loss therein. But 
Mac Linc was called the son of Man for the good cheer of his house and for the 
goodness of the man himself. Mac Coise again was called the son of God for the 
greatness of his charity, and be died on a pilgrimage. 

Now, the name of Mac Liac’s harper was Ilbrechtach (infra, 114); he had for- 
merly been harper to Mac Lonain, and after Mac Lonuin’s death he served Mac Liac. 
Mac Lise went to visit Brian and converse with him, and brechtach went with 
him. He would often go from Loughrea southward across Slieve Aughty to 
Limerick, carrying with him twelve bottles and suitable victuals thereto. For 
there are twelve points of view in Slieve Aughty, and he used to drink a bottle at 
each of them. Once upon a time they went southwards and sat them down on a 
certain hill named Cend Crochain, and Mac Liac said: ‘There be many hills and 











p. 306] NOTES. 588 


lakes and notable places, and ’twere great knowledge to know them all.’ ‘If 
Mac Lonain were here,’ said Ilbrechtach, ‘he would know the story of every spot 
we see.’ Said Mac Liac, ‘Let some one take this fellow and hang him!’ 
Tlbrechtach begged a respite until morning, and it was granted to him; and he 
fasted all that night until the soul of Fland mae Lonain came to his aid. When 
they rose early next morning, they saw Mac Lonain approaching, and he said to 
them: “Let the prisoner go, and I will tell you the story of every notable place 
here in Slieve Aughty.’ So the harper was set free and escaped hanging ut 
Muc Liac's hands, and then Mac Lonain uttered this lay.””

unfortunately the Ms is partly illegible at this point. Cf. Contrib. diupla 
“shelter.”

glan ; see p. 286, 8.

named after Echtach, not after Echtge. Y calls both women Echts, in 10, 11, and 
reads in 16 is ainm don ({s]iiab sliab Eehtgi.

are partly illegible in L.

ina ndoiredhaibh dhathe dosccacilte.

rue a coscur sa comaid: and L 170 a ó has isé dorochair fri Dolb mac Dail; but 1 
do not understand comaid, whereas commdidem is the special term for exultation 
over the head of a fallen foe.

names in lines 45-56, which are only found in Y.

90. tir Mane: the country of Ui Maine comprised parts of Roscommon,

Mag Find is there marked between the Shannon and the Suck, 8.W. of Lough Ree.

Contrib.), where doce means ‘soft’ in the Irish sense, i.e. ‘ moist’ (and therefore 
fertile). Here it is a merely ornamental epithet. 

96. ‘This line is obscure and perhaps corrupt. The meaning may be that the 
wayfarer had enough shrewdness (twaichie) to like to hear poetry gratis. But the 
construction is very awkward, and cennaiges does not rhyme with rocios. Grés is 
applied to any sort of work of art. 

98. O’Curry, MC ii 100, renders this line ‘nor then did he show aught of 
loth’; but perhaps it rather means ‘ there befell him no misfortune therein,’ i.e., 
‘he was in luck'a way.” Coneys, followed by Dinneen, gives diombulaidh “ mis 
fortune, defeat’ (fem.); diombuadh, ‘indignation, displeasure,’ (masc.).

and Hom, 8174,

Ai nduine-taidhe hie Loch Déchaoch i nDeisibh Mumhan. It is recorded a second 
time in almost the same words FM 918. The event is briefly noted in AU 895 
(696). In Chron. Scot. 896, and Ann. Inisf. 896, ap. O'Reilly, Irish Writers lix, 
the murder is ascribed to the Ui Cuirrbuide. There is thus a preponderance of 
testimony, so far as the annaliats are concerned, in favour of a date before the end 
of the ninth century; and O'Donovan assumes that the entry in 918 is an error. 
On the other hand, O’Curry, MC ii 102, quotes an elegy on Ednechin mac Délaig, 
chief of Cinél Conaill, who died in 901 FM (905 AU), which is ascribed to Flann. 
‘This ascription, however, rests, so far as I know, on the sole authority of a 
seventeenth-century manuscript belonging to The O'Conor Don (see MC ii, xii 

In view of the annalistic notices, Y's reading of 122 should be adopted, 
mae Cuirr Buidi, Cond-Géodn. ‘There would then be two murderers vamed, 
Tade mac Faeléin and Cend-Géchn mac Cuirr Buidi. The death of Tade mac 
Feelin, king of Ui Cennselaig, is mentioned in AU 921 (922). Cend Gécáin was 
a king of Cashel; the list in LL 150 5 15 equates him with Fedlimid, who is next 
in succeasion after Cormac mac Culennáin ; but according to AU 900 he is identical 
with Finnguine who was deposed by Cormac in that year, Cend Géchin being merely 
a nickname. The Glossary attributed to Cormac, s.v. Rer, quotes a poem in which 
Mac Lonáin and Cend Géchin are referred to. O’Donovan’s note nd loc. (Corm. 
Transl., p. 146) describes Mac Lonáin as Cend Gécdin’s poet, but this seems to be 
a hasty inference. I should rather suppose the lines to be a citation from a satire 
made by Flann upon Finnguine under his nickname of Cend Géchin: this may 
have been the provocation which led to the poet’s murder. 

123. The meaning seems to be that the murderers made themselves responsible

unaneled.’

ATH CLIATH MEDRAIGE. 


‘Tux name Medraige survives in Maaree, a peninsula five miles south of Galway. 
Ath Cliath Medraige is identified with Clarinbridge, at the mouth of the little 
river which flows down past Athenry to Galway Bay. 

‘The story is connected with Tain Bó Dartada (cf. line 19), but nothing is said 
in the extant versions of that tale as to the fight at the ford or the palisade.

MEDRAIGE. 


Luoaip Mac Cox was defeated by Eogan son of Ailill Olomm in the battle of 
Cenn Febrat, and took refuge in Scotland. He returned to Ireland supported by 
the King of Scotland and other allies from the country of the Saxons and Britons 
to fight the battle of Mag Mucrime: see the tale edited by Stokes in Rev. Celt. 
xiii 449. Our poem enumerates the names of some of those who fought with him 
on this occasion. 

4. Spain is thought of as an island: tr. ‘from a western island—Spain.’ 

16. buide: this may mean either ‘ flaming yellow’ or ‘ yellow with heat’: cf. 
mi buide an a name for August, Contrib. 

35, 43. eride (gen. sing.) is used adjectivally : cf. is mise Oisín ride, Contrib. 

40. Gaillem = Galway. 

57. Rend isin chat) ‘after being wounded in the battle.’ 

64. Bairinn: this is presumably the district in North Clare still known as the 
Burren. 








LOCH RIACH. 


Now Lough Rea in Galway. The bursting forth of the lake is mentioned in 
Airne Fingein among the wonders which attended the birth of Conn Cétchathach 
(Anecd. ii. 6. 2). The personages of the legend belong to the jr side. The text 
of the poem depends principally on L, as the other three copies belong to the least 
trustworthy uss. of the Dindsenchas. M is here worthless, and the scribe of S 
has evidently taken great liberties with his original.

of the lake described in lines 49 ag.

MAG N-AIDNI.

whom Clann Miled brought with them to Ireland. Ai from whom Mag n-Ai was 
called, Femen, Fea, and Fera from whom Mag Femin,|Mag Fea, and Mag Fera are 
named, were also among the number.

MOENMAG. 


‘Ture plain lies east of Mag n-Aidne, round Loughrea: see Onom. Moen was son 
of Allguba and therefore brother to Aidne, but he is not mentioned among the 
twenty-four serfs of Clann Miled in LL 12 ¢ 7. 

7. Labraid, which is B’s reading, is a blunder: it should be labrad, which is 
the reading of BM: Le has Jadvad (a mere error), HS; la). The line is 
parenthetical: render: ‘Moen, powerful hero—a splendid boast that fades not— 
began the lasting use of shaving.” 

11. belaig is gen. dependent on derrtha, and seems here to mean the part left 
bare by the razor. The phrase implies that a fringe is left in front, which points 
to the Roman corona rather than the characteristic native tonsure; see Rhys, Celtic 
Britain, 3, p. 73; Bury, St. Patrick, 239 seg. 

12. maige also depends on berrtha, an instance of zeugma. 

24. dáig bad bladaig: the singular verb is used with a plural subject because 
a verb following ddig is treated as relative: see Atkinson's statement of the 
practice in modern Irish, Proc. B.I.A. ser. 430. 

bith-ndire is gen. ‘in point of generosity.’ 

26. noco tennad: rather ‘he was not mangled for want of science’: Moen was 
a skilful barber. Cf. O'Dav. 1542 tenn .i. letrad. Stokes quotes LL 116 5 19 
rostend cona scin. 

27. selad. O'R has peallad ‘a sight, prospect,’ verbal noun of seilaim. 
Forbarr was no doubt ‘a sight’ after his first shave, 

29. eúan-bla: either ‘ place of troops ' or ' fame of troops.’ 

30. tria is written for trí, as frequently.

may mean ‘lamentable,’ as the clapping of palms, dass-gaire, was an expression of 
grief.

LOCH DERGDEIRC. 


‘Tus story of the naming of Loch Derg is told in the beginning of Talland Etair ; 
see Rev. Celt. viii. 48. The importunate poet is there called Athirne, whereas in 
‘our poem he is Ferchertne mac Athglé. Stokes, Rev. Celt. xxvi. 6, mentions three 
other poets named Ferchertne ; but they all seem to be different from the son of 
Athgló. Another incident from Talland Etair is referred to in the first poem on 
Bend Etair, p. 106, 53, supra, and there also the poet is called Athirne. 

In preparing my collation I accidentally overlooked the copy in 8, which wants 
the first four stanzas, owing to the loss of a folio. Its readings are unimportant. 

3. The meaning assigned to sam-dut is conjectural. 

6. In Talland Etair Eochaid is called King of Descert Connacht ; in Cath Ruis 
na Rig, however, ed. Hogan, § 16-17, he is King of the Clanna Dedad (in Kerry). 
His ‘ Judgments’ are mentioned in the Senchas Mór (Laws i. 18, 23). 

15. eluine is perhaps to be regarded as a subjunctive-imperative after the 
model of euire : see Thurn. Hdb, § 586. 

19. giallaim : literally ‘I,lash’ : cf. fo chosmailius tairb ddsachtaig da ngialland 
@ gal LL 243 54: so BB 435 a 35, 477 @ 10. 

20. I have not elsewhere found réagai/ in this sense. 

. ocus maic: see note on p. 216, 7. 

26. Echach: su all the mss.: it should be Kehdach. 

27. erenach. Cf. airenach in chatha, Ir. T. ii. 2. 16, 1; airenach in tige, 
Ir. T. iv. gloss. ; i n-cirenuch phéine, Fel. Oong. Prol. 118. 

31. ddet dolaid. The bluck chafer is regarded by the Irish with the same 
superstitious horror as attaches in other countries to creatures equally inoffensive, 
the blind-worm, shrew-mouse, salamander, &c. See O'R. baol, “a bug, beetle, 
chafer: a fierce animal.’ Dinneen, baol, ‘a beetle, &c., figuratively, a devil.’ 
Hence the names Dubthach Dael-tengthach, Dubthach Déel Ulad, Ir. T. 
where the compiler remarks is adhsiath le cach nduine in bhéist dara comainm in 
déel d'Jaicsin. 'The word is used metaphorically of a warrior mae Ingoir, déel nar 
dingned, ds. of Cloenloch, 2 (Rennes MS 123 a). 

32. In FM 1157 it is recorded that the head of Eochaid mac Luchta was found 
in that year at Findchora: ‘it was larger than a great cauldron: the largest goose 
would puss throngh the hole of his eye and through the hole of the spinal marrow.’

him nay.’

ni chodail in gerg : it is mentioned in a poem Silv. Gad. i 365 s, along with wood- 
cocks and wild geese. Here it is applied metaphorically to a king.

101-4. This stanza is full of difficulties. gress (quantity uncertain) seems to 
meun sometimes ‘continuance’ or ‘urgency’ in prayer: see Fél. Ueng. Feb. 11 
maith leis gress dia garmaim, Epil. 226 rommain (ronnain) gress a nguide. Stokes 
marks the vowel long in Mart. Oeng., but there is no mark of length in either 
passage in the three texts printed in his original edition. In any case I doubt 
whether gréss alone could mean ‘supplication,’ and the adj. glé-maith points 
rather to the meaning ‘poetic composition.’ ‘Translate, then, ‘ For the King who 
suffered (i.e. Christ) let not my clear noble poem be curtailed ' (i.e. let me not end 
without a pious reflection). Consequently the rendering of mo laith in 103 must 
be altered: does it mean ‘my Prince’ = Christ, as distinguished from the ‘ King 
of the Winds’ = God the Father? In 104 na lamaig is a crux: no manuscript 
has a mark of length on either word; but nd /dmaig seems to be intended: this 
would be for nd ldmaigi (cf. nolessaig Puss. and Hom. Gloss.) ; but the meaning of 
the line remains a puzzle. /dmaigim means ‘1 handle, treat.’ Three Shafts, 
Gloss: cf. BB 237 a 25, 430 ó 44. 

















RATH CRUACHAN. 


Ture is the legend of Ratherogban, the famous home of Ailill and Medb. A group 
of raths is still to be seen by the roadside about three miles west of Tulsk, Co. 
Roscommon. The prose follows closely the Egerton version of Tochmare Ethine, 
§§ 15-16, Ir.T. i. 128. 

2. dumu each dag-niachair : so Temair Luachra is called adba niad is nuachair 
p. 236, 10.

8. i.e. all Erin was bound to pay to the kings of Connacht dues of various 
kinds, such as are enumerated in Leabhar na gCeart. 

10. sit sder-Briain: most kings of Connaught traced their descent either to 
Brian, eldest son of Eochaid Muigmedon, or to his brother Fiachra.

CARN FRAICH. 


Now called Carnfree; it is in the townland of Carns, in the barony and county 
of Roscommon, 3 miles 8.E. of Rath Croghan: see O’Donovan’s note in 
FM iii. 221. 

8. This is corrupt: it wants a syllable.

22. Conall Cernach spent his last days at Rath Cruachan : see the tale Goire 
Conaill ZCP 1, 102, 

Ubend is used of the raised terraces by which a rath built on a large scale 
(prim-dsin) is approached : see LBr. 109a 58, 60 (quoted in Aisl. M.C. glossary). 

25. I do not understand mear, and suggest mir, which is often used metaphori- 
cally: see glossary to Fél. Oeng.

62. oilmeadaig. O'R. has oillmead ‘a balance,’ but ‘even-balanced’ is n

“great at drinking mend’ from mid g.s. meda. In line 102 the same word is 
applied to a place, meaning, perhaps, ‘abounding in mead.’ In the Laws oilmedach 
is a measure of capacity, see Glossary s.v. ól.

29-32. Medb Leth-derg, daughter of Conán of Cualu, was wife first of 
chord, grandson of Conchobar Abratraad: he was killed by Fedlimid 
Rechtmar, who thereupon took Medb to wife: LL 44 5 42-46. Her poetical 
lament over Cachorb is edited by O’Curry MS Mat. 480, from LL 44 6. We are 
told in the same locus that she poisoned Lugaid Laigse at ‘Temair (LL 44 5 48): 
this is no doubt the incident referred to in our poem. The same authority goes 
on to say that she split up the Laigsi and Fothairt into seven divisions, to prevent 
their uniting and to weaken their resistance to the king of Leinster. A Kilbride 
Ms quoted in Silv. Gad. 486 (xv. makes her wife of Art, the son of Conn 
and grandson of Fedlimid Rechtmar.

TURLOCH SILINDE. 


Tuts lake is identified by Stokes with Lough Sheelin, at the meeting-point of the 
counties of Cavan, Westmeath, Meath, and Longford. But it is a long way to 
the east of the places treated of in this part of the Dindsenchas. Besides, the 
prose acrount implies that the name Turloch Silinde was superseded by another 
name, given by the intruder Blonac. Now one manuscript, S (Stowe. D ii. 2), has 
a prose account of Loch mBlonac (sie) which is not included in Stokes’ eilition. 
Loch mBlonac, cid diatá P ninsa. Blonac ingen Tai roaitreb ann, 7 ba ban- 
briugaid amra isidhe: conidh a n-inadh lias a gamhna rofisastair in loch. 
Silend ingen Machair meic Duthain meic Rúin is sí robúi isinn inadh sin ria 
mBlonaic, 7 ba hole re Silind [a] gabail di fuirri co rimgaib hi 7 co forgaib (read 
fargaib) an tir lé co Cuil Silinde u Muigh ‘Ai, conid uaidhe raiter loch Silinde fri 








p. 878 NOTES. 547 


loch Cairrgin 7 Cáil Silinde frisinn inadh ata. “ Loch Blonac, whence its name ? 
not hard to say. Blonac daughter of Tai dwelt there, and she was a famous 
landowner; and it was on the site of her calf-pen that the lake spread forth. 
Silend daughter of Machar son of Duthain son of Run had lived on that spot 
before Blonac came, and she was vexed at Blonac’s taking it from her. So she 
shunned her and left the land to her [and went] to Cuil Silinde in Mag ai. So 
Loch Cairrgin is called Loch Silinde from her, and the spot where she abides is 
called Cáil Silinde.’” It seems then that Turloch Silinde = Loch Blonae, and the 
Loch Silinde (Silend) mentioned in the prose account = Loch Cairgin. This is 
supported by two glosses in LU 66 a and 5, printed in Archiv i. 21: Cuil Sibrinne 
(= Cul Silinne in LL) .i. Loch Carcin 7 0 Silind ingin Madehair roainmniged ; and 
below: Ai Cuil Sibrinne .i. dit hi ft Loch Carrein indiu. According to Hogan 
Loch Cairgin is near Tulsk in Roscommon, south of Elphin. Loch mBlonao is 
mentioned BB 102 a 36, and seems to be near Corcaraige Feda Manach. Fid 
Manach was between Dunmore and Ballymoe to the north-east of Tuam (Onom.). 

9-16. ‘The tenses are future in L, historic in all other ues. 

12. tur: Wi. has tur “a journey,’ on O'Donovan's authority, and this might 
be the meaning here and in 20, but in 16 it gives no sense. I take the word to be 
tur ‘ dry,” here ‘a dry place.’ Cf. TBC (‘Eriu) 1344 Badd ina thur. 

12-16, Translate : “ Silend shall search east and west over every mountain till 
she reach her outlet: Silend, that shall not be a summer stream, visite a dwelling 
whose threshold shall never be dry.’” Here Silend seems to be the name of a river: 
bun means “a river-mouth’: there probably was a place called Bun Silinde. 

sanda (which rhymes with adba) is most iikely derived from sam “summer.” 

tetha: cf. dosth TBC (‘Eriu) 1126, adethim Contrib : it is a future-present. 
ba in 15 and 16 is future. 

Lines 15, 19, and also, no doubt, 3 are octosyllabic, with disyllabie end- 
rhymes (Tai, ersii, &c.). So at p. 380, lines 15, 17, 21, 23. 

18, fo déer-ban dul: cf. M1101 ¢ 6-7 fun dul-so ‘according to this way (of 
interpreting)’; Wb 10 5 20 fon dul toisech ‘according to the first way (of 
interpreting)." 

19. cia rolensat. This means that the lakes faithfully preserved the names of 
the women: lenim is constantly used of a name clinging to a place. Literally 
‘though the waters of the heroic women held fust [to them].” 

20. Literally, ‘it was the toil of one doomed, by reason of her dry place.”

DUMA SELGA. 


‘Tuzs place is now “a green moat to the east of Carnfree’ (Carn Fréich) in Mag 
‘Ai: FM iii 221, note. These bewitched swine must surely be somehow 
connected with the ‘enchanted herd’ of the last poem. They have already 
appeared in the ds. of Belach Conglais: see the note on p. 606. 

2. The Ui Muiredaig or Sil Muiredaig were one of the chief tribes of 
Connaught, including several great families such as ‘the O’Conors, MacDermots, 
Geraghtys, &o., of Roscommon’ (Onom. Sil Muireadaigh). ‘They descended from 
Muiredach Muillethan, who died in 700 (Hy Many, 73). O'Donovan, Hy Many 
138 note, quotes & passage from the Annals of Clonmacnois describing how in the 
fourteenth century a MacDermot ‘ was invested King of Connaught by the twelve 
chieftains of Silemorrie (Sil Muredaig), twelve coworbs and other spirituals,? at 
Carn Fráich.

MAG LUIRG. 


‘Tue full name is Mag Iuirg in Dagda: “it was the name of the plains of Boyle 
that is, of the level purt of the present barony of Boyle (in Roscommon) lying 
south of the river Boyle,” Hy Fiach. 77 note. The legend is taken from Aided 
Ailela 7 Conaill Cernaig, ed. Meyer, ZCP i. 102. 

3. The precise meaning of cuird is uncertain: cf. Metr. Ds. i. 6,23; BB 
273 6 28; Silv. Gad. i. 409, 12; supra, p. 438, 10, and p. 464, 1. 

cnisse may be referred to case ‘curled, tangled,’ or cass ‘ swift.” 

9. Rath Crúachan did not lie in the territory of Cera. Is Cera here a name 
for the Dagda? see Cormac s.v., and cf. fo Chruaich Cera, p. 464, 65. 

.e. at Medb’s words : see ZCP i. 104, 17. 

14. ba Aairde étrén: perhaps ‘it was nobleness robbed of strength’: Conall 
was too old and weak to fuce his enemies. 

15. cen écht mbréithye may mean ‘ without a crime worth words’; cf. ní de 
bladh breithri adevim Ir. T. iv 903. 

16. Mag Slecht is a plain in the barony of Tullyhaw, in the north-west corner 
of Cavan: FMi43, note. The territory of Brefne included the modern counties 
of Cavan and Leitrim (Onom.). Neither echt nor Slecht has a mark of length in 
any of the ss. 

19. bnanna is the reading of L: it does not give a good rhyme, but I can make 
nothing of the alternative drianna, 

20. Ath na Mianna has not been identified; but we learn from Aided Ailella. 
ZCP i 105, 8-10) that Conall was killed at a ford where miners were wushing ore. 















p. 402] NOTES. 551 


The iron-mines of Sliub in Iarainn, just to the east of Lough Allen in Leitrim, 
were close to Mag Slecht: one would therefore look for Ath na Mianna on the 
upper Shannon to the north of Lough Allen. 

21. Martin, The usual form is Mairtine, which stands here in L, but this 
reading will not sean. The rhyme with bale-brig shows that the second syllable is 
treated here as long. The Mairtine belonged to the "Erainn of Munster ; hence 
their desire to avenge Curdi, the hero of the 'Erainn: see ZCP iii. 41, note 5. 
They were settled in Limerick and Tipperary (Onom.) ; in ZCP i. 105, 11, they 
are called the Mairtine of Fermoy (in Cork): the reference to Crích Bérre in 26 
looks as if they were also established south of the Kenmare River. Conall Cernach. 
was Cuchuluinn's foster-brother (Ir. T. i. 142, 18), and as such was involved in 
the blood-feud between Cuchulainn and Curdi, 

25, gléve: ef. note on p. 288, 44. 

31. Goire Cunaill is the name given to the tale which introduces Aided Ailella: 
see 2CP i. 102. 





LOCH Ch. 


Now Lough Key, in the barony of Boyle, in the north of Roscommon. Three 
different prose versions of the legend are found, one in Le, another in S, anda third 
in $:H. The Lecan copy and the poem are printed and translated by Hennessy 
in his introduction to the Annals of Loch Cé, xxxvi seq. This prose version 
supposes that the battle referred to in the poem is the iret battle of Mag Tured, 
known as Cath Maige Tured Conga. But this is plainly an error. This first 
battle was fought close to Cong on the northern shore of Lough Corrib, fifty 
miles to the south-west of Lough Key: so that the druid would never have got 
to the Curlew Mountains, as the prose compiler makes him do, by flying in a 
eouth-eusterly direction from the scene of battle. The Book of Lecan also 
differs from the other prose copies in muking the lake drown Cé in his sleep. 

The prose in H is founded directly on the poem. Here the battle is rightly 
understood to be the second battle of Mag ‘Tured, known as Cath Maige Tured na 
Fomérach. It was fought in the purish of Kilmactranny, in Tir Ailella, at a 
spot somewhere to the east of Lough Arrow, and a few miles to the north-east of 
Lough Key: see Onom. Mag Tuired na Fomorach : D'Arboia, Catalogue 80 : 
Rey. Celt, xii 62. This version will be found in Stokes’ Edinburgh Dindsenchas : 
it is not included in bis supplement to the Rennes Dindsenchas. 

The version in S consists of a few lines only, and gives no fresh details. 

8. imay? forbair : tr. ‘round whom it spread.” 

20. Chuirrsléibe: this is the Irish name of the Curlew mountains. The 
cairn must have been one of the remarkable stone-heaps which are scattered over 
Carrowkeel mountain, though this is not now counted as one of the Curlew 
group. See the paper by RB. Ll. Praeger and B. A. 8. Macalister in B.I.A. 
Proc., January, 1912. 

27. Trunslate: “when he was luid in the cairn of stones.”

LOCH NÉILL. 


Tare was the name of a Inke somewhere in Mag n'Ai (Onom.). 

We have already met the red wine of Drebriu in ds. of Belach Conglais, and 
of Duma Selga. This poem seems to be founded on a somewhat different form 
of the legend. The events of ds. Belach Conglais take place in the time of 
Connire and in de, Duma Seiga Medb appears, who is Conaire's contemporary : 
whereas here we have to do with the son of Enna Aignech, who was king of 
Ireland from A.x. 4888 to 4907 (FM), about 200 yenrs earlier than Conaire 
according to the chronology of the Four Masters. In the beginning of Tochmare 
Ethine (Ir. T. i. 117), Enna Aignech is eight generations earlier than Eochaid 
Airem, who was Connire’s great-grandfather. 

Resides the swine are differently represented. In ds, Duma Selga they are 
persecuted first by a witch-stepmother, aud then by the wife of Buichet: they are 
harniless und amiable creatures, Here they arc mischievous and aguressive. 

But as to the main points of the legend all three poems and that on Ceis 
Choraind aro in agreement. The swine are men nnd women transformed by a 
witch: they are under the protection of Oengus mac ind ‘Oc: they take refuge 
with Buichet of Leinster: they escape from his wife and hide with Drebriu in Sid 
Collomrach : after the lapse of a year they go westward by direction of Oengus 
to the Onk-tree of Tarbga in Roscommon: they are chased by the hounds of 
Medb, and killed at various points in north Connaught. 

5. selgga sldin: the gender is puzzling: aelg so far as I know is always 
feminine: was it originally neuter? The prose calls Nél téiseeh didergach 
“Erenn. 

7. Conall Crom-derg must = Conall Collomrach (i.e. Conall of Collomair) who 
reigned a.w. 4876 to 4880 (FM): he was slain by Nin Sedamain, who was killed 
in turn by Enna Aignech. 

12. ard, cf. note on p. 194, 6. 

romi: this is perhaps to be compared with sufid wain (see note on p. 90, 24], 
which seems to mean ‘be gone from us!” 

22. daire Tharbgai: cf. p. 390, 70. 

25. ba Aord úatha: this should be taken as parenthetic ; translate “ Né1, who 
was a sledge of terror, followed their track ': cf. ord exsoirgne (of a warrior), 
TBC (wi.). 

27. fo thrdig, cf. con tais cen tr 
tide’; (2) ‘exhaustion, mirery.’ 

29. aire: put for airech, to make a thyme.

MAG MUIREISCE. 


‘Turs was the name of a plain in the barony of Tireragh (anciently Tir Fhiachrach) 
to the east of the river Moy. Its extent is accurately defined by O’Donovan, 
Hy Fiach. 267. Muiresc is sometimes treated as masculine, sometimes as feminine: 
in the poem it must be fem. in lines 8, 24; but seems to be muse. in 37 (if the 
reading is correct). 

2, taed-bale: Dinneen has taod ‘impulse, wilfulness’: cf. taodach “sudden, 
inconstant’ : tacdaim “I revolt,’ O'R. Or is the word akin to fóid ‘ radiance’ 
see O'Day. 1578, 

3. bassech, perhaps ‘lamentable’ : see note on p. 336, 31. 

5. déis, g. of dés = dofis, as 6s = sofis. 

9. maigrech is adj. to maigre ‘salmon’: as magar = ‘fry, spawn,” 1 suppose 
that maigre means properly the young fish, the ‘peel.’ Cf. ‘san Mumain 
maigrid, Miss Brooke's Reliques, 270, 1. 

14. tár-gnó : this seems to be a compound of tr ‘ disgrace,’ and gnée (gnó) 
‘beauty’ (but the use. have no mark of length on far). O'CI. has tergné .i 
tdr nó tarcaisne ar ghné. 

16. Translate: ‘the king of every hue (i.e. God) bestowed it of his bounty.” 

16, turehur: turchar ‘riches’ O'R. torchar ‘riches, abundance,’ Dinneen. 
‘The reign of a righteous king was always blessed with plenty: see V. Trip. ii. 
507 note. 

17. romnairn: here and in 43 mairnim seems to mean ‘I inform on, give 
information about’ : our ‘ betray’ shows the sume evolution of meaning. 
take this to be = broce-loimm: cf. sctid iterum in loimm 
Coire Brechin. Meyer, Contrib. would read broce-slawem.

MAG TIBRA. 


Faom the prose version it appears that this was a plain near the mouth of the 
river Moy; it extended from Borrach to the shore (line 46, below), and the 
exact position of Borrach is fixed by O'Donovan, Hy Fiach. 262. 

19. foill: this seems to be the word (adj. or subst. ”) found usually in eo fóill: 
it rhymes with drdin. 

24. Oenach Créachan was the burying-place of the kings of Connaught : see 
Senchas na Relec in Petrie's Eccl. Archit. p. 100 ; according to another document, 
however, the Tuatha Dé did not bury their dead there, but at Brug na Bóinde : 
ibid. 106-7. 

27. co froig : see note on p. 242, 19. 

28. Crích Breis. Mag Tibra is said to be the same as Mag Glass in “Tír Breis 
mac Nenlaidh (Nélaig?): see Onom. : so Crích Breis no doubt= Tir Breis; but I 
cannot find either name in Onom. or Hy Fiach. As the funeral train was 
moving eastward, it must be to the west of Borrach. : 

29. Tromaigis is impersonal. 

36. cor’ chuir: Dinneen gives ‘I bury’ as one meaning of cuipim. 
Cf. p. 456, 72, curfes trén-saithe. I do not know whether the verb can have that 
sense when used absolutely. If so, we must read bein in 36. 

38. Literally ‘she did not put the host in silence’: or possibly ‘the host did 
not bury her in silence.’

CARN CONAILL. 


Sroxs quotes from BB 30 a 22 ay. a version of this legend from which it appears 
‘that Clann Umóir were survivors of the Fir Bolg who had taken refuge in various 
islands off the coast of Ireland and also umong the Cruithne (Picts) in Scotland. 
In this passage and also in the historical story Cuth Cairn Chonaill (ed. Stokes, 
ZCP iii. 203) Carn Conaill is placed in the district of Aidne, as to which see 
introductory note to Mag Aidne. O'Donovan, FM i. 260, suggests that the name 
is now represented by Ballyconnell near Gort in the south of Galway (barony of 
Kiltartan). There is a copy of the poem in the BB 30 a 44 ag. which is not 
included in my collation. It differs from the copy in the BB Dindsenchas, and 
occasionally agrees with L against the other uss. This text is printed with a 
translation by Connellan in Oss. Soc. v. 282. 

14. Rath Chennaig = Rathkenny in the barony of Upper Slane, Co. Meath 
(Onom.). 

Rath Chommair ‘ probably at the confluence of the river Boyne with four or 
five small rivers at Clonard, Meath’ (near Navan, Onom.). 

18. Cnogba Breg = Knowth: cf. de. of Cnogba, p. 40. Brug mn& Elemair = 
Brug na Béinde ; ef. p. 36, 25 ag. 

17. Oenach Tailten = Teltown in Meath. Cermna, not yet identified. 

18. Delete the comma after Tlachtga. Hogan gives na tri Findemna as a 
place-name; but it is really the designation of the three twin sons of Eochu 
Feidlech, Bres, Nár, and Lothar ; Ir. T. iii. 333, Keating ii. 184. 

Tlachtga is identified with the Hill of Ward in Meath. 

19, Divide Ath Sige (Assey, near Tura), Slige Bri Dam (= Bri Dam Dile, now 
Geashill in King’s Co., Onom.). 

26. Render ‘neither more nor less (than four)’: the osthri rath in 37 are the 
same as the curs, and rise means ‘to secure this’: the prose has tucost clann 
Umóir eethra ourw fri sodhoin, 

39. Cet muc Mágach is the great Connaught hero, Mug Méin = Moeumag: 
see p. 334.

30. Ross mac Dedad is a hero of the Erainn of Munster. 

Druimm Cáin : there are several places in Munster so called.

41. Loch Cutra = Longh Cooter in Aidne, Co. Galway, Onom. 

42. Cimloch or Loch Cimme, now Lough Hacket in Galway. 

48. Adur'a home, according to the prose, was in Mag Adair, now Moyare 
between Ennis and Tulla in Clare, Onom.

Muirbech = Kilmurvey in Aranmore Island, Onom.

Tirawley. 

46. Tech Ennaig is not identified. 

47. Rind Bera = Cend Bera, now Kinvarra, near Galway. 

48. Hogan places Modlind in Kiltartan, Co. Galway; but the prose says Mod. 
went to Insi Mod, the islands in Clew Bay. 

49. Cond Buirne (i.e. Head of Bairenn), identified with Black Head near 
Lisdoonvarna in the Burren (Bairenn), Co. Clare. 

50. Iath Aigle is presumably the low country at the bottom of Cruachu. 
Aigle. 

61. Láiglinne perhaps near Loch Láiglinde in Ui mac Cuais (Moygoish in 
Westmeath): see Onom., Loch Léiglinde and Ui mac Ciinis. 

54. Inis Medéin, one of the Aran islands. 

55. Tulach Lathraig is not identified. 

56. Tomanrind = Rind Tamain = Towan Point, a little south of Galway. 

60. Druim n-Asail is in the parish of Croom, ten miles south of Limerick. 
Hogan states that there is a place of the name ‘in Ulaid and in Dal n-Araide,’ 
but this is not supported by the passages which he refers to in Acallam na 
 Senórach. 

62. eaible (L's reading) seems = coible ‘hospitality’: the other texts have 
eaibne = coibne ‘kinship,’ except BB 30, which reads cia bha saidhre, The 
meaning is not very clear.

Roadnacht in triar aile . a.ndumachaib Findmaighe

560 NOTES. [p. 450 


LOCH Ri. 


Tue tale called Aided Echach maic Muireda tells how Eochaid, son of Mairid, 
king of Munster, eloped with his step-mother Ebliu, accompanied by his brother 
Rib (called in this poem Rí). The legend of Lough Ree is there briefly men- 
tioned, and an exactly similar account is given of the origin of Lough Neagh. 
The story is edited and translated (with certain reserves) in Silv. Gud. i. 233, 
ii. 265. Our poem supplies details which are not found in Aided Echach. Thetale 
also told in ds. of Túag Inbir. 

‘M contains a second poem on the same subject, which I have not thought it 
worth while to include in the text, but it may as well be given here :— 


Loch Ri, cred ba failintainm . aeolcha Fail re fir-; 
raidhid ce in Rio fuil . a eolcha dana in domain, 


Ri mac Muireada co mblaidh . do mhuigh Mighi meagar-glain 
dadaghab [read rogab] uitribb and reheadh . a muig n-Airften na n-aingeal. 
Gearrun robo dedla dhe . dambun a baithli a eire: 

darin tipra, bu glan glor . dan mhun mor ina mhedon. 

Leathnaisin tibra tren . tar mag nAirftean [na] n-ard-sgel: 

baithis Ri, bha dedla dhe . eidir each is innile, 

On rig sin ba fortail feidhm . ainmonichear he fo Erinn 

is uadha sin, sloind co moch . ata co dedla in dead-loch. 


Loch Ri, whence comes its name, truly given, O ye learned of Inia Fail? 
Say who was Ri from whom it is called, ye learned poets in all the world ! 


RY, son of famous Muirid, of the bright joyous plain of Meath, got a home 
there for a time in Mag Airbthen of the angels. 

A gelding—the braver was he!—when loosed of his burden staled and made 
8 spring—it was theme of talk—of the abundant flow in mid-plain. 

The copious spring spread over Mag Airbthen famed in story; it drowned Ri— 
the braver was he !—with his horse and all his cattle. 

From that Ri—it was a masterful effort—the lake is named throughout 
Erin: from him—a title early won—bravely arose the noble lake. 

3. This line is used also in ds. of Temair Luachra, p. 236, 11. 

7. For riamna ‘blood-shed’ see Metr. Di 22, 61. 

8. búana gen. of búain ‘reaping’: cf. (rí berta bana ‘three reaped trusses,’ 
Cath M. Léna 80, 3. 

11. trogaig: cf. trog. i. clann; trogais, i, tuamis, Wi. 

13. Hogan says, referring to this passage, that Belach Liao was west of Loch 
Ri, but there is nothing here to support this statement. The travellers came up 
the Shannon in boats (12), and reached Belach Liao (or Belach Da Liao, Silv. 
Gad. i. 234, 6), where they parted. Eochaid going eastward to Meath, and Rí 
westward to Mag Finn (niso called Tir n-Oenaig Midir), which O'Donovan (Hy 
Muny, 77), identifies with Keozh's Country between Lough Ree and the River Suck.

p. 458] NOTES. 561 


19. romairn: cf. note on p. 266, 19. 

a chrotha crá e eri a chrotha : orf is, I think, really an alternative form of criaid: 
dat. or ace. of eré ‘clay,’ cf. scé in line 3, = sciaich dat. of sé. It is usually 
found in Ai ort “in clay,” i.e. “in the mortal body,’ a phrase of Christian origin. 

20. Loch Lindmuine = Loch nEchach (Lough Neagh): see ds. of Túag 
Inbir, Rev. Celt. xvi. 152. 

24. romascul: cf. muscul ‘onset (P)' Rev. Celt. xiii, 122. 

26. The sense of co réenaib robreth is very doubtful. 

28. Icannot explain the allusion, Oenach Uengussa 1s said by Hogan to be in 
Mag Find, but also to be near Fremainn, which is near Lough Owel, a long way 
to the east of Mag Find. 

29-30. The construction is very awkward; it seems = in shiag sin do gilt 
4 mbó. 

32. trí tráth: in the first and second hours their cattle and horses were 
destroyed: in the third they themselves would have perished if they had not taken 
warning. 

33-4, Punctuate after gndth, and delete colon after eét-trdth: a mb, &., is 
governed by romaró in 36. 

41. brothlach is properly “a cooking-pit’: for the metaphor ef, deoch tonnaid. 

47. Fuait : I take this to be a place-name, but it cannot well mean Sliab Fuait 
(the Fews mountains) . 

48. lidedain : O'C1. explains this word .i. erwidh no elanna. 

60. co mér-chaitte: see note on p. 348, 15, 

55. O'Cl. has the gloss muat .i. mullach, ‘ar mual an mhaighe’ i. ar mullach 
an mhaighe. 

56. ergaire: cf. LU 58 5 37 ergaire mér-slsiaig. 

58. imchoss: Ihave not met this word elsewhere, but the meaning is deter- 
mined by the context. 

62. trachlad: Peter O'Connell’s Dict. has trochladh .i. sgaoileadh no truaill- 
eadh: ct. trochailte “ worn out,’ Dinneen. 

68. ón seems to be = din, tiain: the word is written Asian in Ml. 28 d 12 (so 
‘Thurn. Hab. p. 74). 

77. cen vigne: perhaps ‘ without restraint.’ 

81. Rogab fond fo thalmain = rogab fotha fo thalmain in the prose. 

82. fuargg : I keep L’s reading, as it cannot well be a scribal error : it is more 
probably a rare word, for which the other copies have substituted the usual term 
fúal. I have not seen it elsewhere, 

93. cen raind “ without division,” i.e. Ri and every one in his house. 

95. fo thrdig thai: a metaphorical expression for the silence of death.

LOCH ERNE, 


Lugs 49 to end of this poem were edited and translated by O’Beirne Crowe in 
R.I.A. Irish MSS. Series, 18764p. 186. 

1. This line is quoted by O'CI. s.v. oscar, with the gloss is ard lingens ad. 

4. find-liain : perhaps ‘ white-mooned,’ but ef. 34. 

6. Fiachu Labraind (usually Labrainde), king of Ireland, 3727 to 3761. 

15. brath baeguil treachery that gets an opportunity’: for daegui in the sense 
of ‘an opening, an unguarded moment, a chance to injure some one,’ see Contrib. 

17. em raind, i.e. over all Ireland. * 

23. ní rosemairn meace: this seems to mean that, in spite of strong drink, the 
bard’s memory has not betrayed him into confusing the names. 

24-26. The river Flesk keeps it name; the Mand is now the Muine: both are 
in Kerry; so the Labrann may also be there: sco FM 3751, note a. 

26. samlam: cf. note on p. 274, 55. 

28, tairm dar trocha (of. 76) literally ‘a sound (fame) beyond short ite.” 

29-36. As to these battles see FM a.u. 3751. 

31. Béirre = Berehaven, south of the Kenmare River. 

35. rothatlaig: perhaps from *to-ad-tiuehur, which would give Mid. Ir. 
tatlaigin, as *ad-tiuchur becomes atlaigim. The same verb is used in a short 
poem on Fiachu’s reign, LL 18.04 = Lee. p. 63 a 33 = H. 1. 15, p. 100. 

Rothatlaig tria cheilg . i cath Gatlaig gairg 

mae Eochach inn airm . Mafemis, ainm n-airg. 
O'CI. has tathlaigh nó rothathiaigh .i. docheannsaigh ‘he tamed’ (tatailcim), which 
would suit the line just quoted, but not our passage. 

39. na rusdilgenn: this a strange form, but has much more authority than 
nachas dilgend. . 

40. It is not obvious why Fiachu, who was styled King of Beirre in 31, should 
here be called lord of Fobar, which is in West Meath. 

49. chuird, see note on p. 396, 3. 

51. sdrgad is «contracted form of sdrugua: cf. Silv. Gad. i. 73, 37; ibid. 361 y. 
‘The line seems to refer to the opprobrious name Buredach ‘the Bellower’ and to 
mean ‘ the name (sound) was an insult to the noble...’: I can make nothing of 
othrin, 

53. cen nemain ‘ without venom,’ cf. LL 213 ó 21 tue doib nemain ocus neim, 

57. Translate ‘She had in keeping to her credit’: the prose calls her ban- 
chométaid do chiraib > do chliórib Meidbe. 

64. doith ‘sullen, forbidding’: uf. dothfer, p. 380, 29. 

65-8. tuidbsin (for Caidbsin) is nom, to roscaindre. 

T cannot explain the form of gairbsin. 

fo chair, literally “ under reproach.’
